issue_title: $ENVIRON can't access environment variables created in a fixture
issue_body:
Can:
`env GABBI_VERBOSE="False" python -m unittest test_loader`

Can't:
fixtures.py
```python
class GabbiVarFixture(fixtures.GabbiFixture):
    <-- snip -->

    def start_fixture(self):
        try:
            os.environ.get('GABBI_VERBOSE')
        except KeyError:
            os.environ['GABBI_VERBOSE'] = "False"
```
test_thing.yaml
```yaml
fixtures:
    - GabbiVarFixture

defaults:
    verbose: $ENVIRON['GABBI_VERBOSE']
<-- snip -->
```
I'm sure I'm doing something wrong, and I know environment context can be infuriatingly tricky in Unix from dealing with it in crontabs, but I finish my work day with this conundrum. I'll do some research over the weekend and see if I can figure my way out of this problem.

Also, interesting:
If I use an $ENVIRON['var'] in my YAML settings for the booleans, like $ENVIRON['GABBI_VERBOSE'] and it loses the value due to the above problem, it will always evaluate as True. (default: False, so I think it's the $ENVIRON replacer throwing in a wrench)

issue_body:

issue_title: The eager read/write optimization leaves curio programs highly susceptible to event loop starvation
issue_body:
There was some discussion in #83 about removing the "eager write" optimization that curio does, where `await sock.send` will attempt to synchronously write to the socket directly, and only yield to the event loop if that fails. It turned out that the original reason we were considering removing it didn't actually apply, plus it significantly slows down the echo server microbenchmark, so for now the optimization survived.

But... I've just realized that actually the eager read/write optimizations create a serious correctness issue, in that they can cause ordinary-looking code to starve out the event loop in realistic situations.

Here's a [standard curio echo server](https://gist.github.com/njsmith/d6bb8d7fb517f6248b9fb940294d63bf), along with a particularly aggressive echo client. The test case is thta we run two copies of the client, and watch to see how well the server switches between servicing both of them. The server is instrumented to report how often it switches.

The server output pretty regularly contains lines like:
```
Switched after 220 loops (100.7 ms)
```
What this is saying is that one of the `echo_handler`s managed to complete 220 passes through its read/write loop before yielding to the kernel, so all other tasks were starved out for 100.7 ms.

I even saw a
```
Switched after 340 loops (171.6 ms)
```
without trying particularly hard. And in theory the only limit on how high those numbers can go is that at some point some scheduling quirk or something on my laptop causes one of the clients to be slow to read/write and makes the server block; under the right conditions it could go arbitrarily high.

@glyph has some [interesting comments on this optimization in Twisted](https://github.com/python/asyncio/issues/446#issuecomment-256498678), and how they removed it.

There's also an important difference between curio and twisted: in twisted, reads are *only* triggered by callbacks, so they always go through the event loop; it's only writes where you can even contemplate doing the eager-write optimization. So for them, this starvation problem is less likely to happen, because you can only hit it if you're doing an endless series of writes without any reads in between (and as glyph says, "/dev/urandom as a service has pretty limited utility"). In curio, we currently have *both* eager-read and eager-write optimizations, which makes the problem much easier to hit -- all kinds of realistic protocols are susceptible, starting with echo servers and proxies and moving on to things like an HTTP/1.1 server talking to a pipelining client.

I'm particularly worried about this because as you know I've been poking at HTTP server support for curio, and it's completely unacceptable for a fast client to totally lock up the server for unbounded amounts of time, even if it's a low-probability event :-(. (I.e., I don't think this is something where tweaking some heuristics will be enough; we need to able to actually put a hard bound on how long a task can monopolize the kernel.) It can be worked around by manually inserting calls to `curio.sleep(0)` (now that [that's fixed](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/commit/b144a5d1ae6e0cdaf8f8b7c67fb44bcf82912bfa)), but that's not at all a nice solution in general.

The obvious solution is to make sure to always unschedule-then-reschedule on reads and writes. I can't immediately think of any cleverer (= faster) solution that is guaranteed to actually work.

issue_title: getCall is not working correctly
issue_body:
```
spy = sinon.spy()
spy(1)
spy(2)
spy.getCall(0)
spy.getCall(1) # fails with "IndexError: The call queue only contains 1 calls"
```

I think that `getCall` is not actually returning a call, but rather returning a queued spy/stub. Every time a new spy or stub is created, a new item is added to the queue, and this new item is what's returned by `getCall`. What is supposed to happen is that `getCall` should return the _execution_ of a particular spy or stub. Perhaps `args_list` should be used by `getCall`?

issue_title: Preceding '\n' at Table serialization
issue_body:
Hi,

I wonder: what is: https://github.com/avakar/pytoml/blob/master/pytoml/writer.py#L123-L124 for? I recently started to use your library and I've been wondering (until I've been taking a peek into the source) how come that when using tables there's always an "unnecessary newline" prepended.

Knowing the "TOML"-spec, my initial guess was:

> (...) Tables (...) appear in square brackets _on a line by themselves_ (...)**[1]**

Yet, isn't this covered by all the appended '\n's in all calls to io.StringIO() [specifically assigned to 'fout' in pytoml's context]?


Thanks for your work and best regards,
Patrick

**[1]** - https://github.com/toml-lang/toml#table

issue_title: Add method to extract features in PCA-family algorithms
issue_body:
Is that possible to add a method to PCA-algorithms to extract the important features?

I like the approach for classical PCA, but struggle how to adapt to methods from **mlxtend** library
```
def get_important_features(transformed_features, components_, columns):
    """
    This function will return the most "important"
    features so we can determine which have the most
    effect on multi-dimensional scaling
    """
    num_columns = len(columns)

    # Scale the principal components by the max value in
    # the transformed set belonging to that component
    xvector = components_[0] * max(transformed_features[:, 0])
    yvector = components_[1] * max(transformed_features[:, 1])

    # Sort each column by it's length. These are your *original*
    # columns, not the principal components.
    important_features = {columns[i]: math.sqrt(xvector[i] ** 2 + yvector[i] ** 2) for i in
                          range(num_columns)}
    important_features = sorted(zip(important_features.values(), important_features.keys()),
                                reverse=True)[:k_best]
    print "Features by importance:\n", important_features
    return [feat[1] for feat in important_features]
```
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/48844159/how-to-extract-columns-names-from-rbfkernelpca?noredirect=1#comment84692998_48844159 - or is that correct?

issue_title: Automatic versioning breaks if mercurial repository is synced with a GIT upstream through hg-git extension
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Armin Gruner (BitBucket: [ArminGruner](http://bitbucket.org/ArminGruner), GitHub: [ArminGruner](http://github.com/ArminGruner))**

----------------------------------------

Hi,
if a local hg repo is pushed/pulled towards a GIT repository through the hg-git extension, bookmarks are used to synchronize which branches are pushed upstream:

```
#!shell
» hg tags
tip                               99:989ec00b2a11
default/master                    99:989ec00b2a11
```

Unfortunately, this breaks the check in hgtools/versioning.py:

```
#!python

if tags == ['tip'] and not self.is_modified():
        tags = self.get_parent_tags('tip')
```

hgtools always assumes the local sandbox has been modified. I've changed the code to just check whether the sandbox version is the tip-most version if the reported tags contain the "tip":


```
#!python

if 'tip' in tags and not self.is_modified():
        tags = self.get_parent_tags('tip')
```

Not sure whether this does not break any corner case, though..

Regards
Armin

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/hgtools/issue/31

issue_title: PEP8 Compliance
issue_body:
PEP8 support is always a very nice-to-have, especially with a project receiving contributions. Fortunately `netmiko` is almost there with the following exceptions:

Flag | Issue
-------|--------
E303 | Too many blank lines
E501 | Line length > 79
F401 | Module imported, but never used

Looking at errors for the E303, it's because methods are consistently double-spaced where they should be single spaced within a class (global functions are double spaced).

E501 wouldn't be too tough to fix, a lot of what I saw was pretty straight forward.

F401 is a simple fix. It complains because `__init__.py` imports modules to be exported, but is never used beyond that. Since this is for the purpose of exporting, `__all__` should be set to a list containing these objects.

I can help/fix these if there's interest in making it conform to Python stylistic standards. :)

issue_title: Plucking a little more complex example
issue_body:
I am working with this dictionary:
```python
[{'id': 'abcd', 'reviews': [{'rating': 5.0}, {'rating': 3.0}]},
 {'id': 'cdab', 'reviews': [{'rating': 2.0}, {'rating': 1.0}]}]
```
and want to get all the reviews out.

If I try it with `plucks(obj, '*.reviews.*.rating')`, the output is an empty list. If I take a step back and do `plucks(obj, '*.reviews')`, the result is:
```python
[[{'rating': 5.0}, {'rating': 3.0}], [{'rating': 2.0}, {'rating': 1.0}]]
```
It's also interesting to note that it works if I say: `plucks(obj, '*.reviews.0.rating')` and the output is `[5.0, 3.0]`. 

What am I missing here?

issue_title: improve baselevel interpolation
issue_body:
Using interpolation on the edge of the map together with a ``pixelbuffer`` causes issue #69.

Furthermore, using ``pixelbuffer`` is also inefficient because 9 output tiles instead of 1 have to be opened every time.

A solution could be to add a pyramid without ``pixelbuffer`` which replaces both internal (process & output) pyramids when executing calls with the ``baselevel`` flag.

issue_title: Version information
issue_body:
We currently only store the version in `setup.py`, which is not very useful. There should be a switch `nbstripout --version` and/or `nbstripout version`.

Options for implementing this include:
* [Create a `version.py` module and load in `setup.py` via `execfile`](http://stackoverflow.com/a/2073599) (would require turning `nbstripout` into a package)
* Use [versioneer](https://github.com/warner/python-versioneer)

Any thoughts @mforbes @michaelaye?

issue_title: Fix midnight() and naive() references on Quickstart page
issue_body:
The [overview](http://delorean.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) mentions that 

- _Delorean.epoch_ is a property, not a function.
- _Delorean.midnight_ is a property, not a function.
- _Delorean.naive_ is a property, not a function.
- _Delorean.timezone_ is a property, not a function.


but the [quickstart page](http://delorean.readthedocs.org/en/latest/quickstart.html) still has examples showing `midnight()` and `naive()`

Hello there!

This issue is more likely a `help wanted` than anything. 
I'm writing [PyFunceble](https://github.com/funilrys/PyFunceble) (in this issue I'm referencing to the `dev` branch) and since more than 2 months, I'm trying to find why colorama is not working under a specific circumstance.

So back to our issue:

# Case that it is working

As PyFunceble is running for more than 6 months every day under Travis CI, server and sometimes PC, I consider the following as safe to mention.

## Locally

If I run `PyFunceble` (the executable) locally it's working. 

Same if I create a script like the following, it's working.

```shell
#!/usr/bin/bash

PyFunceble -d github.com
```
## Travis CI

Under Travis CI if I run `PyFunceble` directly for testing, it is working as you can see in the [following link](https://travis-ci.com/funilrys/PyFunceble).

# Case that it is not working

## Locally

No case found after months of testing and using.

## Travis CI

If we create a `.py` file or a bash script, and run `PyFunceble` from inside the script, it is not working.

Examples:

* All 50+ Travis CI builds of https://travis-ci.org/Ultimate-Hosts-Blacklist (.py)
* All 15+ Travis CI build of https://travis-ci.org/dead-hosts (.py)
* https://travis-ci.org/mitchellkrogza/Phishing.Database (bash script)

___________
Maybe I forgot to configure something, but I'm still not understanding this issue...

So, does anyone know what am I missing and why it is not working?

Thanks in advance for taking the time to read this issue and for the time you may take to answer this issue.

Have a nice day/night.

Cheers,
Nissar

issue_title: duplicate tags should be filtered out
issue_body:
sometimes tags in mpd protocol can be duplicates of one another.
This esp. happens if one tagged his library using beets.

This is how raw protocol serves those tags:
```
currentsong
file: ogg/Brandi Carlile/2008 Creep Live/01-Creep.ogg
Last-Modified: 2015-04-30T02:18:24Z
Album: Creep Live
Title: Creep
Track: 1
Artist: Brandi Carlile
Date: 2008
AlbumArtist: Brandi Carlile
Genre: Rock
Track: 1
Time: 273
Pos: 13
Id: 181945
OK
```

in python-mpd2 the 2 "track" values will be represented as one string value. 
It would be better if such values would actually shown as key:value, basically eliminating the duplicate.

This isn't a bug-report per say, but given that you said, "If you find any part of the documentation lacking...", I'll ask my question here.

Looking at my local pointer for reference, the first 50 bytes after the function pointer is
```python
>>> numpy.ctypeslib.as_array(ctypes.cast(simple_function.address, ctypes.POINTER(ctypes.c_uint8)), (50,))
array([ 72, 184,   8,  48,  86, 228, 229, 127,   0,   0, 242,  15,  88,

```
Only the first three bytes (72, 184, 8) and the (0, 0, 242, 15, 88, 0, 195, 0) section are consistent from run to run, with the 8-byte string coming right before a representation of 3.14 (31, 133, 235, 81, 184, 30, 9, 64). I see this starting on byte 117 in the ELF (labeled "right there").

So it's in there.

On to pyelftools:
```python
>>> from elftools.elf.elffile import ELFFile
>>> from io import BytesIO
>>> elf = ELFFile(BytesIO(elfbytes))
```

The last section has the symbol table:
```python
>>> list(elf.iter_sections())

```
and it has the expected symbol names:
```python
>>> [x.name for x in list(elf.iter_sections())[-1].iter_symbols()]
[u'', u'simple_function', u'', u'__main__.simple_function$1.float64', u'cfunc.__main__.simple_function$1.float64']
```
One of those three is the function I want.

I see that these `Symbol` objects have an `entry` dict-like, and some of these are numerical offsets, but how do I get the offset from the beginning of the ELF?

That is, if I load the ELF bytes into a Numpy array, how do I use the information from pyelftools to construct an offset relative to the base address of that array? (In other words, how do I do `dlsym` with pyelftools?)

(This ELF doesn't seem to be compatible with the normal `dlopen`/`dlsym` mechanism: writing to a file and doing `ctypes.cdll.LoadLibrary` results in `OSError: /tmp/dummy.so: only ET_DYN and ET_EXEC can be loaded`.)

issue_title: Socket module doesn't return ipv4 client connected to tcp6 socket
issue_body:
Hello,

tcp6 socket actually works for both ipv4 and ipv6. See [section 3.7 - Compatibility with IPv4 Nodes of RFC 3493 - Basic Socket Interface Extensions for IPv6](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3493#section-3.7).

Thus, when a daemon only creates a tcp6 socket, it can be used by ipv4 client. For example, the gerrit server only creates a tcp6 socket, and ipv4 clients looks like this in netstat:

```bash
$ netstat -nea | grep 29418
tcp        0      0 192.168.0.109:53594     192.168.0.109:29418     ESTABLISHED 994        44153592  
tcp6       0      0 :::29418                :::*                    LISTEN      995        44154634
```

This behavior makes it impossible to look for gerrit clients with testinfra Socket module:
* host.socket("tcp://:::29418") works but  .clients returns empty list
* host.socket("tcp://0.0.0.0:29418") or "tcp://29418" doesn't works

It seems like "tcp://$port" should works when there is any tcp6 socket.

issue_title: Certain Unicode character cause an unusual progress bar error
Module name may not be sufficient for Python profiles
issue_body:
Python profiles are sometimes hard to read because all you see is:
- module name
- line number
- function/method name

but you don't see
- package name
- class name

There may be a dozen different modules with the same name in different packages, don't let me get started about methods called `__init__`.

Now it's hard to do anything about class names if the profiling data doesn't contain that information (at least on Python 2.7; Python 3 introduced `__qualname__` that gives me hope for the eventual bright future, although I haven't actually checked if the profiler records those).

Package names are also difficult, since all we have is filenames.  (If the files still exist you could walk up the tree looking for `__init__.py` files: when these stop, you found the root package.  Except Python 3.3 breaks that heuristic with its implicit namespace packages and anyway, you cannot always rely on having the source code available -- what if the profile was made on a different machine?).

Still, not all hope is lost: the full filename is available, and humans can figure out the package name from that (or look up the class name from filename + line number).  Filenames are long, though, and would not fit in the graph nicely -- but wouldn't it be awesome if they were available via tooltips?

Certain graphviz formats (such as SVG) support tooltips.  I have a patch prepared.

issue_title: pytest-flake8 is very slow and uses lots of RAM with -vv
issue_body:
If you run py.test with --flake8 and -vv then the tests run **very** slowly and it also uses a huge amount of RAM.

This is caused by flake8's extreme amount of debug sent stderr when run with -vv, which is in turn captured by py.test.

A workaround is to set -vv via addopts config which isn't detected by flake8 but still applies to py.test.

In testing with just 8 files this reduced the time to run from 16 seconds to just 1.

issue_title: Thread support in signal mode
issue_body:
Your README says 

> On Mac/Linux/Unix, pyinstrument can run in 'signal' mode. [...] However, this can only profile the main thread.

You could instead sample the call stacks of all active threads by using [sys._current_frames()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys._current_frames), which returns a dict mapping thread IDs to frame objects.

issue_title: Consider rewrite of command line interface to Click
issue_body:
I am considering rewrite of command line UI into `click`. We could benefit from it in multiple places:

# Simpler testing (no mockup for stdout/stderr needed)

Click provides option to write test cases, capturing stdout without a need to mockup stdout stream.

Our test cases would get simpler and would probably has less issues with stdout differences at python 2 and 3.

I had recently few issues with existing stdout mockup so I skipped some test cases, which would be handy.

# Support for subcommands

We have three subcommands. Current implementation uses `argparse`, which allows subcommands too, but is not very clear and easy.

Issue #45 (Abort in case of unknown option) would be resolved by that.

# Safer output with python 2 and 3 with `click.echo`

Using `click.echo` we could prevent some nasty problems with python 2 and 3 and console encodings. `click.echo` is like `print`, but does it's best to prevent differences in both pythons.

It is likely, it would resolve the issue #44 (Exception Unicode...)

# Configuration from files or env variables

It would be very easy to have options configurable from env variables. Support for application configuration file is also present.

# Color output with autodetection if console is color capable
We can output with colors. This might be an issue on terminals, which are not color capable.

Click allows autodetection of terminals being unable to handle colors.

# Bash autocompletion

Click allows generation of autocompletion script for bash (no other shells supported at the moment).

# Easy support for paging long outputs

Our output can get multiple pages long. It is easy to support paging where pagers are available.

Following is short test, how it works:

    import click                                                                                       
                                                                                                       
                                                                                                       
    def gen_lines(count, suffix):                                                                      
        tmpl = "{i}: {suffix}"                                                                         
        for i in range(count):                                                                         
            yield tmpl.format(i=i, suffix=suffix)                                                      
                                                                                                       
                                                                                                       
    @click.command()                                                                                   
    @click.option("--count", default=300)                                                              
    @click.option("--suffix", default="some more line content")                                        
    def page(count, suffix):                                                                           
        """Command printing multiple pages of some text. Testing paging"""                             
        click.echo_via_pager("\n".join(gen_lines(count, suffix)))                                      
                                                                                                       
                                                                                                       
    if __name__ == "__main__":
        page() 

# Conclusions

I feel, we could benefit from converting CLI from `argparse` into `click`.

In case there is chance to get such pull request accepted, I can write it.

The plan is as follows:

- convert all CLI from `argparse` to `click` (preserve existing features)
- rewrite test cases to use `click` test case support.
    - resolve issue #45 at the same time
    - modify all `print` into `click.echo`. Make sure, we get issue #44 resolved

This shall conclude this issue.

Following features should be left for further modifications:

- configure command by env. variables
- configure command by application configuration file
- terminal color support autodetection
- pager support
- bash autocompletion

Let me know, if there is chance for such PR to be accepted.

issue_title: Library can report incorrect error messages
issue_body:
Consider the following schema:

```python
s = Schema(
    Any(
        {Required('field'): In({'field_a', 'field_b'}), Required('value'): str},
        {Required('field'): In({'field_c', 'field_d'}), Required('value'): int},
    )
)
```

If I call `s({'field': 'field_a'})` I get:

- `voluptuous.MultipleInvalid: required key not provided @ data['value']`

If I call `s({'field': 'field_c'})` I get:

- `voluptuous.MultipleInvalid: value is not allowed for dictionary value @ data['field']`

The first of these makes sense, the second doesn't because `'field_c'` is a valid value for `'field'` in at least one alternative in `Any`.

This may be tricky to solve in the general case. When calling with ``s({'field': 'field_c'})` there are two errors for the first option (invalid "field" value and missing "value" field) and only one error for the second option (missing "value" field). So it may be possible to present the error message associated with the "least bad" match.

Consider this wikicode:
```

```

MediaWiki parses this as a plain text, but mwparserfromhell gives an invalid URL (`http://`) plus plain text (`>hostname</phpMyAdmin/`):
```python
In [1]: import mwparserfromhell

In [2]: wikicode = mwparserfromhell.parse("http://>hostname</phpMyAdmin/")

In [3]: wikicode.nodes
Out[3]: ['http://', '>hostname</phpMyAdmin/']

Out[4]: ['http://']

In [5]: wikicode.filter_text(recursive=False)
Out[5]: ['>hostname</phpMyAdmin/']
```

I guess the parser should check if there is at least one valid character after `http://`...

issue_title: Trusted Advisor refreshes
issue_body:
This was opened to combine #30, #31 and #32:

We want a CLI option to handle refreshing Trusted Advisor limit checks. This will require and additional IAM permission.

The possible options should be:

1. (default, current behavior) do not refresh TA checks
2. Refresh all limit-related checks, and wait for them to finish refreshing.
3. Refresh all limit-related checks but do not wait for them to finish; just trigger the refresh and continue on. Data will likely still be stale when queried. This is mainly useful when awslimitchecker is cron'ed, and you're OK with TA data from the _last_ run.
4. Refresh limit-related checks that are more than X seconds old, and wait for them to finish refreshing.

See [refresh-trusted-advisor-check](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/support/refresh-trusted-advisor-check.html)

issue_title: Please add a dummy cache backend for API compatibility.
issue_body:
The docs say your Django cache backend adds a convenient `.lock(...)` function (which is great). But we need to use a dummy cache backend in some cases (e.g. building a Docker image or running tests) when no Redis service is available or otherwise necessary.

If our application code uses this convenient `.lock(...)` function, it will break when we switch the backend to Django's provided dummy cache backend unless we program defensively around every usage.

With the normal dummy cache backend, I think it just never returns a hit. With a dummy `.lock(...)` function, I suppose it should always successfully acquire a (dummy) lock?

issue_title: LIBUSB_ERROR_NOT_SUPPORTED when trying to perform isochronous transfer
issue_body:
Hi. I'm trying to perform simple isochronous IN transfer on existing sound card device.
IN endpoint is located on interface 2, alternate setting 1.

I'm using Windows, libusbk backend. I've already tried WinUsb driver with the same result (driver is installed on root composite device using Zadig).

This is what I'm doing so far:
1. Get libusb context (context = usb1.USBContext())
2. Get device handle (using context.openByVendorIDAndProductID)
3. handle.claimInterface(2)
4. handle.setInterfaceAltSetting(2, 1)
5. Create new transfer (handle.getTransfer(100))
6. transfer.setIsochronous() - endpoint ID 0x81, transfer length: 17600, iso_transfer_length_list is empty.
7. transfer.submit()

Unfortunately, call to submit throws LIBUSB_ERROR_NOT_SUPPORTED [-12] exception. I'm not sure why, as isochronous transfers should be supported in libusbk backend?
Do you have any examples how to perform isochronous transfer on non-zero interface and alternate setting? It would be very helpful to solve that issue.

issue_title: CachedLoader breaks application
issue_body:
Hello!

We are using django-mobile in our project.

We use Django 1.9 and experienced trouble with cached loader: it ruined the application down with exceptions:

1. `./manage.py runserver` just crashed when tried to set flavour to mobile
2. `uwsgi` has a maximum recursion depth exceeded when tried to set flavour to mobile

Our templates configuration is like this:

templates/base.html
templates/mobile/base.html

mobile/base.html has {% extends "base.html" %}

In this situation problem started to appear.

We had to turn cached loaders at all, because django-mobile's loader is broken and Django's cached loader do not working with django-mobile.

As far as i see, the problem is in django-mobile's CachedLoader implementation — it does not understand what current flavour is, so it generate broken cache key for both base.html and mobile/base.html, which looks like this:

full:base.html

So the mobile template tries to extend "base.html", but gets itself content (with extend) instead of original, root base.html.

issue_title: Password reset does not work when no password exists
issue_body:
I am not sure if this is an issue with redux or the underlying Django code. 

I have been bashing my head trying to get a password reset email to send with no luck and I was thinking that it was my email backend that was having issues but it was not. 

I was trying to reset a password for a user that never had a password set because the user was created programmatically without one. I figured this out by going through the whole auth process from start to finish and then trying the password reset and it worked. When I looked at the difference of the two records I noticed the test record had no prior password. Seeing this I manually set a password and then tried the password reset and it worked. 

I have tried digging through source code but I cannot find any where in the password reset process where it checks if an old password exists. 

I think returning an error, or at least some notification, would be nice so people like me do not spend hours debugging a bizarre issue. I would be happy to help if someone can point me in the right direction to do so.

issue_title: Avoid weak RNG unless user explicitly permits it
issue_body:
I think it would be a good idea to avoid falling back to weak RNG (`random.Random`) unless the user has explicitly expressed that they are OK with it. This is on the idea that it is often better for the command to fail than to (unknowingly) get a weakly random result. 

In case of normal evocation, lack of secure random (`random.SystemRandom`) would cause the command to fail. The error message can instruct the user to try again with e.g. `--allow-weak-rng` option if they are OK with weak random.

I am willing to implement this if you think it is a good idea.

issue_title: Consider using wrapt with decorators
issue_body:
Now that we're using `@api_call` with all of our methods, they no longer can be introspected properly. For example, see http://box-python-sdk.readthedocs.io/en/latest/boxsdk.object.html#boxsdk.object.base_object.BaseObject.get .

We could potentially use wrapt (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/wrapt) to fix this. See https://github.com/GrahamDumpleton/wrapt/tree/develop/blog for more info.

Though this would potentially slow down the SDK code, so it might or might not be worth it. Perhaps it can be enabled when generating docs or using the terminal, but disabled otherwise.

issue_title: Need a clear and thorough tutorial for writing custom operators in c++ with nnvm
issue_body:
[This page](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/how_to/new_op.html) gives a very simple tutorial for creating operators in c++. After reading, however, I'm still unclear how to write a complicated operator in c++.

Compared to other frameworks (like caffe, pytorch), learning how to create operators in c++ is indeed much harder for mxnet/nnvm. A lot of new concepts, classes and macros are involved, and not explained in detail: 
1. For Attribute Parser, what's NodeAttrs? What's the type of attrs->parsed? What field types are supported by DMLC_DECLARE_FIELD and how? Why I cannot find ParamParser and other classes in the [C++ API](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/doxygen/annotated.html)?
2. What does it mean by "Outputs can be made invisible to other operators"?
3. For FGradient, what's NodePtr and NodeEntry? Why the prototype is so different from FCompute? No example is given for how to write a FGradient function and how to access NodePtr and NodeEntry.
4. For FCompute, what's TBlob? What is the difference of TBlob and NDArray/mshadow::Tensor? How can I know which input blobs need gradient and how can I tell which output blobs need gradient?
5. The existence of req argument in FCompute makes writing custom operators more difficult, although it may have some benefit for performance. Is it possible that I only handle the case of kWriteTo and the framework handle the other cases for me?
6. What's auxiliary states and how to use that?
7. How to declare backward dependency and request resource like the legacy operator interface?
8. For python, what's the lifetime of an instance of operator? Will an instance of operator be called more than once in a forward pass of a graph? For c++, how can I implement a stateful operator that has some internal states (possibly a very complicated c++ data structure in cpu memory) that are accessed across many forward and backward passes (but do not need to be saved)? And how to initialize those states?

Overall, the nnvm c++ API for creating custom operators is really complicated, especially compared to caffe. A thorough tutorial is required. Or better, simplify the API and make it easier to understand.

Thank you.

issue_title: util.inherits and requires use inheritence, breaking the MRO
issue_body:
As discussed in on [the mailing list](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/luigi-user/7m0uZkyyYow), using @inherits messes up the method resolution order and breaks `super()`

```
>>> class Foo(luigi.Task): pass
...
>>> @inherits(Foo)
... class Bar(luigi.Task): pass
...
>>> Bar.__mro__
(<class '__main__.Bar'>, <class '__main__.Bar'>, <class 'luigi.task.Task'>, <type 'object'>)
```

Since Bar is now twice on the list, any calls to super will result in infinite loops.

This problem comes from @inherits using inheritance to modify the class. We should look into whether the class can be monkey-patched instead of subclassed, so that inheritance works as expected.

issue_title: ASSIMP_{BIN,INCLUDE,LIB}_INSTALL_DIR variables are used not correcty
issue_body:
If we set one or all of `ASSIMP_{BIN|INCLUDE|LIB}_INSTALL_DIR` variables like follow:

```
$ cmake -DASSIMP_LIB_INSTALL_DIR=lib32 ..
```

then **CMake** will add `${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARIES_DIR}` value between `CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX` and `ASSIMP_*_INSTALL_DIR` in the generated **cmake_install.cmake** file. So if our build directory equal to `/home/user/prog/build` then libraries will be installed into `${DESTDIR}/home/user/prog/build/usr/lib32` directory.

To fix this issue I can suggest following patch:

```
--- assimp-3.2-orig/CMakeLists.txt	2015-11-03 21:47:37.000000000 +0300
+++ assimp-3.2/CMakeLists.txt	2016-07-06 14:42:59.944796851 +0300
@@ -91,11 +91,11 @@
 ENDIF ( CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR STREQUAL CMAKE_BINARY_DIR )
 
 # Cache these to allow the user to override them manually.
-SET( ASSIMP_LIB_INSTALL_DIR "lib" CACHE PATH
+SET( ASSIMP_LIB_INSTALL_DIR "lib" CACHE STRING
   "Path the built library files are installed to." )
-SET( ASSIMP_INCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR "include" CACHE PATH
+SET( ASSIMP_INCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR "include" CACHE STRING
   "Path the header files are installed to." )
-SET( ASSIMP_BIN_INSTALL_DIR "bin" CACHE PATH
+SET( ASSIMP_BIN_INSTALL_DIR "bin" CACHE STRING
   "Path the tool executables are installed to." )
 
 IF (CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE STREQUAL "Release")

```

Also in the **assimp.pc** file the exec_prefix defined not correctly and this issue can be fixed by following patch:

```
diff -b --unified -Nr assimp-3.2-orig/assimp.pc.in assimp-3.2/assimp.pc.in
--- assimp-3.2-orig/assimp.pc.in	2015-11-03 21:47:37.000000000 +0300
+++ assimp-3.2/assimp.pc.in	2016-07-06 14:42:59.944796851 +0300
@@ -1,5 +1,5 @@
 prefix=@CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX@
-exec_prefix=@CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX@/@ASSIMP_BIN_INSTALL_DIR@
+exec_prefix=@CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX@
 libdir=@CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX@/@ASSIMP_LIB_INSTALL_DIR@
 includedir=@CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX@/@ASSIMP_INCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR@/assimp

```

This deploys correctly, we have 1 master & 3 nodes available in vCD.

issue_title: Add clear error message if invalid mode is configured
issue_body:
Currently, it is possible to pass any gibberish to `paypalrestsdk.configure` as `mode`, and it will happily accept it. Creating payment with such gibberish mode doesn't work, obviously.

Minimal example:

    paypalrestsdk.configure({
        "mode": "gibberish mode",  # offending line
        "client_id": settings.PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID,
        "client_secret": settings.PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET
    })
    payment_info = {
        "intent": "sale",
        "payer": {"payment_method": "paypal"},
        "transactions": [{
            "item_list": {"items": [{"name": "item",
                                              "sku": "item",
                                              "price": "1.00",
                                              "currency": "USD",
                                              "quantity": 1}]},
            "amount": {"total": "1.00",
                              "currency": "USD"},
        }],
        "redirect_urls": {"return_url": '/',
                                  "cancel_url": '/cancel/'}
    }
    payment = paypalrestsdk.Payment(payment_info)
    payment.create()

The code above yields a following error:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
    File "/projects/paypal_example.py", line 36, in <module>
        payment.create()
    File "/python_env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/paypalrestsdk/resource.py", line 163, in create
        new_attributes = self.api.post(self.path, self.to_dict(), headers, refresh_token)
    File "/python_env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/paypalrestsdk/api.py", line 272, in post
        return self.request(util.join_url(self.endpoint, action), 'POST', body=params or {}, headers=headers or {}, refresh_token=refresh_token)
    File "/python_env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/paypalrestsdk/util.py", line 22, in join_url
        url = re.sub(r'/?$', re.sub(r'^/?', '/', path), url)
    File "/python_env/lib/python2.7/re.py", line 155, in sub
        return _compile(pattern, flags).sub(repl, string, count)
    TypeError: expected string or buffer

I think it would be better to fail earlier, say, in `Api.__init__`, and throw something like `ValueError: incorrect mode; expected "sandbox" or "live"` instead of spilling internals like that.

I understand that docs explicitly specify that only "sandbox" or "live" settings can be used, but readable error message is always an improvement.

issue_title: Future Improvement: Deprecate error param and use message
issue_body:
10 of the functions in Selenium Library use the keyword name `error` while the majority use `message` to set a custom error message.

The following functions use `error`:

* Wait For Condition
* Wait Until Element Contains
* Wait Until Element Does Not Contain
* Wait Until Element Is Enabled
* Wait Until Element Is Not Visible
* Wait Until Element Is Visible
* Wait Until Page Contains
* Wait Until Page Contains Element
* Wait Until Page Does Not Contain
* Wait Until Page Does Not Contain Element

Not high priority, but something to just keep in mind. I will update the contributing document to say to use the `message` attribute for future work on the library. Some of these keywords are probably fairly popular so care must be taken to not break backwards capability.

issue_title: _intercept_std_streams() does not play nice with python/robotframework logging
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, the way how logging works in combination with the PythonRemoteServer depends very much on when the corresponding logging StreamHandler is first created.
If it is created before _intercept_std_streams() is called, then the logging output will be sent to stderr of the server. If it is created after _intercept_std_streams(), then the output is sent as `output` to the calling client.

I fixed this for my use case with this change to _intercept_std_streams(), which makes sure that I receive all log messages logged during run_keyword() on the robotframework client:
``` python
    def _intercept_std_streams(self):
        sys.stdout = StringIO()
        sys.stderr = StringIO()
        # reset the logging handler so that it is connected to the right stderr stream
        import logging
        logging.getLogger().handlers = []
        ch = logging.StreamHandler()
        logging.getLogger().addHandler(ch)
```
NOTE: I mostly opened this issue so that it is documented in case other people run into similiar problems. I'm not sure if this is something that can/should be handled by the default implementation of PythonRemoteServer, since you would have to consider a myriad of possible python logging configurations. Feel free to close.

issue_title: HalInitialize should be called when hal is imported
issue_body: There's a lot of things that will segfault if HALInitialize isn't called. Given that it's ok to call HALInitialize twice, I think we should call it in the module, to make sure people don't accidentally segfault their robot. That could be the cause of http://www.chiefdelphi.com/forums/showthread.php?p=1425864, for example.

issue_title: Git dependency with sepcified branch gets overriden by dependency od other package.
issue_body:
This issue is simillar to #1779, but the solution stated there is not working for me.

I need to install specific version of a `PyInstaller` package, but `PyInstaller` is also a dependency of `PyUpdater` which is also required in my project and overrides other my specific `PyInstaller` version.

<details><summary>`$ python -m pipenv.help` output:</summary>

`python -m pipenv.help`

`C:\Program Files (x86)\Python36-32\python.exe: No module named pipenv.help`


I am aware this is not how this output should look like, but I can not make it work. Any help here? I'm on pipenv version 11.0.2.
</details>

------------

##### Expected result

I need latest `develop` branch of PyInstaller installed and I don't want PyUpdater to override this with the `PyInstaller-3.3.1` version. As suggested in #1779 I placed PyUpdater above the PyInstaller in `[packages]` in Pipfile.

##### Actual result

Regardless whether the important part of Pipfile looks like this:
```
PyUpdater = "*"
PyInstaller = {git = "https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller", ref = "develop"}
```
or like this:
```
PyInstaller = {git = "https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller", ref = "develop"}
PyUpdater = "*"
```
the PyInstaller version in Pipfile.lock will be `3.3.1` and I end up with this version installed.

What is interesting is that if I uninstall PyInstaller with `pipenv uninstall pyinstaller` and then install it again with `pipenv install https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/archive/develop.zip` I get the `Successfully installed PyInstaller-3.4.dev0+b31794cf1` but in fact the `3.3.1` version in installed instead.

##### Steps to replicate
```
# Pipfile
[[source]]

url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]
PyUpdater = "*"
PyInstaller = {git = "https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller", ref = "develop"}
```
`pipenv install`
Check version: `pipenv run pyinstaller --version`

or

Create new clean environment
`pipenv install pyupdater`
`pipenv uninstall pyinstaller`
`pipenv install https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/archive/develop.zip`
Check version: `pipenv run pyinstaller --version`

issue_title: Change `pipenv install` to update the lock file first
issue_body:
(Breaking out another substep of #1255)

When a specific package to install is passed to `pipenv install`, the current logical flow is as follows:

- for each package requested on the command line:
    - install the package into the current environment
    - if that fails, abort the operation
    - update `Pipfile` with the new or revised dependency
- after all requested packages have been installed, regenerate the lock file (as long as `--skip-lock` was omitted)

This creates some opportunities for the environment and the lock file to become inconsistent with each other:

- the individual install commands don't use the lock file as a source of constraints, so they may install different versions of dependencies from those that would be installed when using the lock file
- because the installation happens before the lock file generation, there's a race condition where the lock file generation may see a newer release than the install operation did
- installing individual packages will resolve environment markers inside the virtual environment, while lock file generation will resolve them outside the virtual environment (this point means we may need to resolve #857 before we can safely make the logic change proposed below)

The proposed change to the installation logic would be as follows:

- for each package requested on the command line:
    - check that the requested package is available from one of the source repositories listed in `Pipfile`
    - if it's missing, abort the operation
    - don't make any changes to `Pipfile` until *all* the requested packages have been checked
- for each package requested on the command line:
    - update `Pipfile` with the new or revised dependency
- update `Pipfile` with the new pre-release setting (if `--pre` is specified)
- assuming all requested packages are available and `--skip-lock` was omitted, regenerate the lock file
- run `do_init` (just as the command already does for the case where no packages are specified, which means this approach also already handles the `--ignore-pipfile` and `--skip-lock` options)

issue_title: Support for requiring packages at install time
issue_body: Is there support or planned support for installing packages *before* installation the current package (at build time), such as Cython or NumPy, as described in https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/

issue_title: Please consider lowercase: pipfile and pipfile.lock
issue_body: Is there a good reason why `Pipfile{,.lock}` couldn't be all lowercase? It sucks to have to hit the SHIFT-key when using the commandline, IMHO. At least, please consider accepting both `pipfile` and `Pipfile`.

I can circumvent the trouble by uninstalling setuptools just after creating a new virtual environment.
In this case, the version of setuptools I've installed into system packages becomes visible again.

issue_title: The default function in HashingPartitioner is wrong
issue_body:
https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2cb39da37356d3a73554325a663915698d4dbb97/pykafka/partitioners.py#L53-L61

Bad Python form. If you want anything resembling consistency, `hash` should never be used. There's no guarantee that it's going to produce consistent results between architectures and interpreter restarts. Instead, it should be using sha1 or, even better, mmh3, as the default.

issue_title: Q: BalancedConsummer's commit_offsets() does not prevent from message double reception?
issue_body:
Hi, 

I've a balanced consumer that reads a flow from kafka.
At some point, e.g. every 1 second (for test purpose) I execute the code like:
```python
...
     consumer.consume() # note: non-blocking call, timeout 100ms
...
            if (time()-last_ts) >= 1:  # every second, for the example
                self.consumer.commit_offsets()
                self.consumer.stop() #?
                reconnected += 1
                log.warning("Recreating the consumer for the %d st/nd/th time", reconnected)
                self.connect(self.connect_retries)
                last_ts=time()
...
```
(i had to do it because of the failures when having long-running kafka consumer script) 

the self.connect looks like:
```python
        self.connect_retries = connect_retries
        ntries = 0
        self.kafka_client = None
        log.info("Connecting to kafka...")
        while (self.kafka_client is None): 
            try:
                self.kafka_client = pykafka.KafkaClient(hosts=self.kafka_hosts)
            except Exception, e: 
                t, v, tb = sys.exc_info()
                ntries +=1
                if (ntries > self.connect_retries) and (self.connect_retries != -1):
                    raise t, v, tb
                log.exception("Could not connect to kafka: %s. Retrying in 1 second. Try %d/%d.", str(e), ntries, self.connect_retries)
                sleep(1)

        log.info("Looking for a topic %s...", self.topic_name)
        while self.topic_name not in self.kafka_client.topics: 
            log.error("Could not find topic at the kafka brokers. Updating info and retrying in 1 second")
            sleep(1)
            self.kafka_client.update_cluster()
        self.topic = self.kafka_client.topics[self.topic_name]

        log.info("Creating a balanced consumer...")
        self.consumer  = self.topic.get_balanced_consumer(
               consumer_group=self.consumer_group, 
               zookeeper_connect=self.zoo_hosts, 
               consumer_timeout_ms=timeout_ms,
               auto_commit_enable=True)
```
Each consumed message in a test message set had unique id; and what i do with them then received, is inserting in a database. When I have this reconnection code, I got duplication primary key errors, meaning the consumer processed some messages twice (or more)

Could you please clarify if it is what is expected to happen? 

Thanks

issue_title: Should BalancedConsumer.consume() block/fail if zookeeper connection is pending/dead?
issue_body: This comes out of #206. Clearly there are use cases where, if the zookeeper connection goes away and so you can't know if you still own your partitions, you should not be consuming from them. That's probably the safest option, ie to block/fail completely, and is what's the core of this commit (which would need redoing because it depends on a lot of other commits on that branch): https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/commit/3b23fadeacfec18b91d22aaf121a349a329d02cc

issue_title: Wrong error message when certificate verify fails
issue_body:
I was debugging an issue with setting up my connection and the error message I got was:

'Unable to connect to a broker to fetch metadata. See logs.'

So I enabled debug logging and indeed, some more context was given there:

```
2017-07-12 14:25:06,311 Failed to connect to 123.123.123.123:9093
2017-07-12 14:25:06,311 Failed to connect newly created broker for 123.123.123.123:9093
2017-07-12 14:25:06,311 Socket disconnected during metadata request for broker 123.123.123.123:9093. Continuing.
```

After looking for some time, I found out that this is not a metadata issue, but my (SSL) CA file was incorrect, which resulted in a certificate verification error: [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed

But this error wasn't logged... So here are the steps that I took to find out:

I was looking into this issue and trying to find out what was going wrong here. I found all three messages in the source code (respectively connection.py, broker.py, cluster.py). I noticed that in connection.py in the function def connect two errors are caught in one line:

`except (self._handler.SockErr, self._handler.GaiError):`

And when one of those 2 errors happen, the following will be executed:

```
log.info("Failed to connect to %s:%s", self.host, self.port)
raise SocketDisconnectedError
```

So that's my first logmessage. Then the SocketDisconnectedError is raised and caught by the Broker constructor:

`log.warning("Failed to connect newly created broker for {host}:{port}".format(host=self._host, port=self._port))`

That's my second logmessage and the constructor just continues. So where does the 3rd logmessage comes from: def _request_metadata in cluster.py:

```
except SocketDisconnectedError:
   log.error("Socket disconnected during metadata request for "
       "broker %s:%s. Continuing.", host, port)
```

So if the Broker constructor has caught the SocketDisconnectedError itself, some other line must raise a SocketDisconnectedError also. So the call to broker.request_metadata is the one that also raises such error. And indeed the @_check_handler doest this.

_check_handler raising a SocketDisconnectedError is fine, but logging "Socket disconnected during metadata request" is not completely right. Because request_metadata is not reached because of @_check_handler. So I was figuring out what was wrong in metadata context... but I found out something else was going on.

Drilling down the code I came again at the lines in connection.py:

```
def connect(self, timeout):
    """Connect to the broker."""
    log.debug("Connecting to %s:%s", self.host, self.port)
    try:
        self._socket = self._wrap_socket(
            self._handler.Socket.create_connection(
                (self.host, self.port),
                timeout / 1000,
                (self.source_host, self.source_port)
            ))
    except (self._handler.SockErr, self._handler.GaiError):
        log.info("Failed to connect to %s:%s", self.host, self.port)
        raise SocketDisconnectedError
    log.debug("Successfully connected to %s:%s", self.host, self.port)
```

No actual errormessage is logged... so I logged the SockErr itself:

```
    except (self._handler.SockErr as, self._handler.GaiError) as e:
        log.info("Failed to connect to %s:%s %s", self.host, self.port, str(e))
```

And found out that this is going on:

Failed to connect to 123.123.123.123:9093 [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:661)

So I would like to suggest to log this str(e) error so you exactly know what's going on.

issue_title: Incompatibility with supported Django versions
issue_body:
Tox tests against Django 1.5 up to Django 1.8
* https://github.com/emory-libraries/eulxml/blob/master/tox.ini#L12

Those Django versions are not longer supported by the Django project team and receiving security fixes.
* https://www.djangoproject.com/download/

Only Django 1.11 and 2.x are supported at the moment.

However eulxml is incompatible with these versions: it uses a function, `get_declared_fields`, that has been deprecated and removed in Django 1.9

I believe we need to:

1. Determine what Python and Django versions we want to support:

I suggest dropping support for the following versions of python:
* 2.6 (python2 users are encouraged to migrate to 2.7)
* 3.3 (it has reached [end of life](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0398/#x-end-of-life))

I suggest dropping support for django 1.5, 1.6, 1.7, 1.8 and 1.9 and supporting only 1.10, 1.11, 2.0.

1.11 and 2.0 are the only officially supported versions.

issue_title: Loading truncated images (difference between repl and script)
issue_body:
## Description

When loading a big geotiff. I noticed that in a python script `skimage.io.imread` would fail with:

```
OSError: image file is truncated (55156 bytes not processed)
ValueError: Could not load "/home/cmcdragonkai/Projects/image-numpy-conversion/images/2.tif" 
Reason: "image file is truncated (55156 bytes not processed)"
Please see documentation at: http://pillow.readthedocs.org/en/latest/installation.html#external-libraries
```

However the same code works when just using the python repl.

The solution was to use:

```
from PIL import ImageFile
ImageFile.LOAD_TRUNCATED_IMAGES = True
```

Which is sort of a leaky abstraction. My dependency is on `scikit-image` not pillow. Perhaps this code should be put into `scikit-image`?

But also I'm wondering why there's a difference in behaviour between repl and script.

Scikit version: 0.12.3 Python 3.6

issue_title: Python wheel build does not create platform-specific binary files
issue_body:
CC @rcrowder @scottpurdy 

Running `python setup.py bdist_wheel` should create a platform-specific binary `.whl` file in `bindings/py/dist`, but it actually creates a generic file. This is because all the C building is being done in CMake now, so distutils doesn't know it contains a C extension.

issue_title: Remove PROJECT_BUILD_RELEASE_DIR CMake option in favor of CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX
issue_body:
With numenta/nupic.core#170, the `PROJECT_BUILD_RELEASE_DIR` user option is deprecated in favor of the standard `CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX`.  This task is to remove support for it entirely.

Where a user may currently optionally specify an alternate installation path thusly:

`cmake -DPROJECT_BUILD_RELEASE_DIR=<PATH> ../../src`

The user will be required to do the following instead:

`cmake -DCMAKE_INSTALLPREFIX=<PATH> ../../src`

issue_title: BTrees 4.4.1 manylinux wheels broken for Python 2.7 - no C extension
issue_body:
Installing the [BTrees 4.4.1 manylinux wheels for Python 2.7](https://travis-ci.org/zopefoundation/zope.app.container/jobs/224724078#L323) doesn't get you the C extension.

This shows up as a [build failure](https://travis-ci.org/zopefoundation/zope.app.container/jobs/224724078#L641) by anything that *needs* the C extension. Notably, because of zopefoundation/zope.security#20, security checks on `iter(tree.items())` fail.

These wheels should probably be deleted from PyPI.

(Unfortunately they can't be re-uploaded again.)

issue_title: pandas_datareader or pandas-datareader, pick one single spelling
issue_body:
The fact that there are two different spellings for this package has caused me some confusion

My suggestion is to go with "pandas_datareader", as this is the spelling that works with python (import pandas-datareader is not valid syntax). This means that this git repo as well as all the documentation should refer to the package as "pandas_datareader".

Even if renaming this repo is messy, I do suggest to make the changes in the documentation here http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/remote_data.html

issue_title: Revalorisation annuelle des plafonds des ressources au 01/04/2018 - ASPA, ASI, MTP et +
issue_body:
TLDR : quelques chiffres et plafonds à modifier, tout dans dans la circulaire, merci :)
cc @guillett @sandcha @ThibaultCCMSA 

Au 1er avril 2018, les montants et les plafonds de ressources relatifs à l’Aspa bénéficient d’une
revalorisation exceptionnelle prévue par le décret n° 2018-227 du 30 mars 2018 portant revalorisation de
l’allocation de solidarité aux personnes âgées, pris en application de l’article 40 de la loi de financement
de la sécurité sociale pour 2018.

La revalorisation applicable au 1er avril 2018 aux autres prestations est de 1 % (coefficient de 1,01 -
Instruction n° DSS/2A/2C/3A/2018/77 du 19 mars 2018) et s’applique aux avantages suivants :
- Allocation aux vieux travailleurs salariés (AVTS), secours viager et allocation aux mères de
famille (AMF) et majoration L. 814-2 du CSS (abrogé au 1er janvier 2006) ;
- Allocation supplémentaire d’invalidité (ASI) ;
- Majoration pour tierce personne.

L’application combinée de la revalorisation exceptionnelle de l’Aspa et de la revalorisation de 1 % a pour
effet d’actualiser les montants suivants :
- Allocation supplémentaire ;
- Limites de récupération des sommes versées au titre de l’Aspa et de l’ASI.

Source : http://www.legislation.cnav.fr/Documents/circulaire_cnav_2018_10_05042018.pdf

issue_body:
If a 'tranche_n' threshold is above 'tranche_n+1' one's an overlap may occur.

On my machine I've got :
```

print simulation.legislation_at(periods.instant("2015")).ir.bareme
OUTPUT :
MarginalRateTaxScale: bareme
- 0.0  0.0
- 9690.0  0.14
- 26764.0  0.3
- 71754.0  0.41
- 151200.0  0.45
- 151956.0  0.45
```
Last tranche should not be present.

The param.xml is like this :
```
      <TRANCHE code="tranche4">
        <SEUIL>
          <VALUE deb="2013-01-01" fin="2013-12-31" valeur="71397" />
          <VALUE deb="2014-01-01" fin="2014-12-31" valeur="151956" />

The 2013 tranche 5 is taken into account for 2015, that should not be...

issue_title: Bigtable support for microsecond timestamp precision
issue_body:
Currently Bigtable only supports millisecond timestamp precision:
https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/reference/admin/rpc/google.bigtable.admin.v2#google.bigtable.admin.v2.Table.TimestampGranularity

My application expects microseconds, and I've had to make some clunky bug-prone adjustments to shoehorn it into working with milliseconds. It would be great to have microsecond precision.

issue_title: No way to override default deadlines in Spanner API
issue_body:
Currently none of the Spanner APIs expose a way to override default deadline. This is especially important for read and query apis since people can use those APIs to stream millions of rows and the default deadline (which I think is [30 seconds](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/spanner/v1/spanner_gapic.yaml#L109)) is just too small for those cases.
One way of doing this would be to allow users to pass in gax CallOptions.

issue_title: Default sudoers doesn't allow run as any user
issue_body:
The template for adding new entries in accounts.py is:

```sudoer_lines.append('%s ALL=NOPASSWD: ALL' % user)```

but should probably be:

```sudoer_lines.append('%s ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL' % user)```

to allow users to "sudo -u [username]" without being prompted for a password. This functionality is desirable because "sudo -u" is the default command that ansible uses when switching to a new user during provisioning. Also, the lack of this line in sudoers doesn't seem to add anything security-wise since you can still successfully execute "sudo su - [username]" without being prompted for a password.

issue_title: elasticloadbalancingv2 LoadBalancer validation bug.
issue_body:
I encountered a glitch with the validation of the  LoadBalancer class for elasticloadbalancingv2.  The error occurred when attempting to allow for the creation of either aninternal or internet-facing NLB from the same template.  When trying to generate the template, I received an error instead:

`ValueError: LoadBalancer: only one of the following can be specified: SubnetMappings, Subnets`

The NLB definition causing the error is:

```
    NetworkLB = template.add_resource(elb.LoadBalancer(
        "NetworkLB",
        Name="NetworkLB",
        Scheme="internet-facing",
        Subnets=If(
            "isInternal", 
            [Ref(subnetA), Ref(subnetB)], 
            Ref(AWS_NO_VALUE)),
        SubnetMappings=If("isInternal", Ref(AWS_NO_VALUE), [
            elb.SubnetMapping(
                AllocationId=GetAtt(eipA, 'AllocationId'),
                SubnetId=Ref(subnetA)
            ),
            elb.SubnetMapping(
                AllocationId=GetAtt(eipB, 'AllocationId'),
                SubnetId=Ref(subnetB)
            )
        ]),
        Type='network'
    ))
```
Since this is a valid template, I propose we remove the [validation](https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/master/troposphere/elasticloadbalancingv2.py#L143-L148) from this class, and leave it to the template creator to ensure they are not setting the properties incorrectly.  I will also get a PR submitted shortly.

issue_title: Bundle behaviour change for directories in v2
issue_body:
The `v2` engine currently captures matched directories in `Snapshot`s, but doesn't actually propagate them through to `FilesetWithSpec`. One effect of this is the `jvm_app` `bundles` that explicitly refer to directories don't actually end up recursively including the directory content.

The {{fileset}} args here are matching directories, rather than files, and the semantics of {{fileset}} are ill defined. The following bundle fileset:
```
bundle(fileset='some/example/directory/')
```
...will not match any files in v2. But changing the glob to:
```
bundle(fileset='some/example/directory/**/*')
```
...will recursively match all files under the directory, similar to the v1 engine.

----

I think there are two changes to make here.

1. We should include all matched paths in the FilesetWithSpec in order to support the existing bundle case.
2. We should add a deprecation warning for bundles for the case where a bundle matches _only_  directories. The idea is not to ban matching directories in the long term (that's totally reasonable)... instead, the goal would be to try to detect cases where people were expecting everything under a directory to be recursively included by a `fileset`, and instead ask them to change to an explicit recursive glob if that's what they wanted.

issue_title: Travis not sanity checking contrib module consumption
issue_body:
As part of #4992, I adjusted travis to build all modules on the binary builder shards, but to skip actually running the tests for all contrib modules (because, in particular: the android and node contrib modules take a long time to test, and the tests run on other shards).

But it appears that we lost coverage related to whether a module is actually importable as a plugin (as opposed to being loadable when it is located directly on the `PYTHONPATH`), and so as of 7c92a6f8d1ec7397b14c41bcd3519fc0d14ea624, running `build-support/bin/release.sh -n` fails with something like:
```
Exception message: 'module' object has no attribute 'build_file_aliases'
Failed to install and test package pantsbuild.pants.contrib.confluence-1.4.0rc0+0c69e778!
Dry run release failed.
```

I think a middle ground might be to simplify the "test that a plugin is usable" blocks significantly, but to remove the `-c` flag, and run sanity checks on the binary builder shards.

issue_title: Add Android support to the Pants build system
Add flags to configure remote execution
issue_body:
This code: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/9319f7f0d91e00b9b470ff1d43d861b208bb2157/src/rust/engine/src/context.rs#L66-L98 currently hard-codes 5 important parameters. Instead, we should have the following flags:

`--remote-store-thread-count`
`--remote-store-chunk-size-bytes`
`--remote-store-chunk-upload-timeout`
`--process-execution-parallelism`

And we should set the `remote::CommandRunner` thread count to... Probably `--process-execution-parallelism` + 4 or something, so a few background threads can live.

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5881 is an example PR that added some flags and passed them through to the same place.

issue_title: Kolibri should explicitly set cookie name
issue_body:
<!--
Instructions:
 * Fill out the sections below, replace …'s with information about your issue
 * Use the 'preview' function above this text box to verify formatting before submitting
-->

### Observed behavior

When running Kolibri and Studio at the same time in the same browser, Kolibri will immediately sign the user out and require them to sign in again. This process will repeat for as long as both apps are running.

This happens because Kolibri and Studio both currently use the Django default cookie name, 'sessionid'. This causes Kolibri and Studio to set each other's sessionid after various actions.

### Expected behavior
When you sign in, you stay signed in, Studio or not. :)

### User-facing consequences
This is primarily an internal developer issue, though it could happen on devices that deploy other local services in addition to Kolibri that are using Django and keep the default cookie name.

### Steps to reproduce
- Start Studio and sign in
- Start Kolibri and sign in
- Return to Kolibri and try to perform an action, like importing content
Result: It says you are signed out and asks you to sign in again. Once you sign in again, this process will repeat.

### Context
- Latest Kolibri from develop branch as of 6-28-18
- Latest Studio from develop branch as of 6-28-18
- OS X 10.13 (High Sierra)
- Any browser, tested with Safari, Chrome, Chromium, and FIrefox

issue_title: add more metadata to InteractionHistory of an exercise
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

In #3012 it was observed that if an question in an exercise failed to render due to an error, it counted as 'correct' to a learner.

In #3483 we switched the behavior so that an error causes the question to be marked as 'incorrect'.


### Expected behavior

Neither behavior above is optimal, because the learner has either 'correct' or 'incorrect' on their record due to something out of their control (the question having an error).

Instead, we should add more metadata to the `InteractionHistory` of the exercise which allows us to distinguish more about what happened. For example, it could be 'took a hint', 'error' occurred, etc.

See this thread: https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/3483#discussion_r179900439

### User-facing consequences

learner is penalized for something out of their control


### Context

0.9.x

issue_title: SummaryLogs are not summarizing - new one created for each session.
issue_body:
## Summary

SummaryLogs are not accumulating progress over sessions, see an example of three consecutively created SummaryLogs, for the same user/channel/content combination.

```
    {
        "pk": 8,
        "user": 3,
        "content_id": "c1fef81ed45543cda589c53db04e8a9f",
        "channel_id": "838ca10504b4470db20767208aebf133",
        "start_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:16:40.307000Z",
        "end_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:16:43.481000Z",
        "completion_timestamp": null,
        "time_spent": 1472231803.481,
        "progress": 0.0,
        "kind": "audio",
        "extra_fields": "{}"
    },
    {
        "pk": 9,
        "user": 3,
        "content_id": "c1fef81ed45543cda589c53db04e8a9f",
        "channel_id": "838ca10504b4470db20767208aebf133",
        "start_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:16:43.628000Z",
        "end_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:17:00.687000Z",
        "completion_timestamp": null,
        "time_spent": 17.206,
        "progress": 0.0,
        "kind": "audio",
        "extra_fields": "{}"
    },
    {
        "pk": 10,
        "user": 3,
        "content_id": "c1fef81ed45543cda589c53db04e8a9f",
        "channel_id": "838ca10504b4470db20767208aebf133",
        "start_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:17:00.790000Z",
        "end_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:17:01.199000Z",
        "completion_timestamp": null,
        "time_spent": 0.512,
        "progress": 0.0,
        "kind": "audio",
        "extra_fields": "{}"
    },
```

This is happening because the `idKey` static property of the ContentSummaryLog (and incidentally, the ContentSessionLog) are not currently set to `pk`, but are setting to the default `id`. However, in the serializers.py, we are sending the id field as `pk`.

Flagging @jamalex @jayoshih here to alert to issue. But this will be fixed in a PR I will be submitting later today.

## System information

*Please specify the Kolibri version you were using and your operating system!*

 - Version: MVP
 - Operating system: Linux
 - Browser: Chrome

## Traceback or relevant snippet from server.log or browser console

```
INSERT TRACEBACK, LOG MESSAGES ETC. HERE
```

## How to reproduce

1. Engage with a piece of content while logged in.
2. Navigate away.
3. Navigate back.
4. Engage again.

## Real-life consequences (anything community should be aware of, for instance how it affects your deployment)

SummaryLogs are not working as intended, and are currently doing no more than SessionLogs.

issue_title: Feature Request: segregated user classes
issue_body:
From the Security Model page:

> 
> What an authenticated user can achieve
> A user can view all secrets.
> A user can view all secret to service mappings.
> A user can create new revisions of a secret.
> A user can create new revisions of a service mapping.
> 

It would be grand to be able to have classes of users:

- admins, who have all power
- users, who are tied to the enumerated services by the admins (possibly through farming out to some LDAP system or other RBAC provider).

IOW, applying the principle of least privileges to the users.

issue_title: Single tag constraint causes JujuAPIError
issue_body:
When a constraint spec is given to deploy or in a bundle with a single tag constraint (e.g., `tags=node1234` rather than `tags=node1234,othertag`) it causes the following exception:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/conjureup/controllers/deploy/common.py", line 24, in do_deploy
    await app.juju.client.deploy(fn)
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/juju/model.py", line 1186, in deploy
    await handler.execute_plan()
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/juju/model.py", line 1780, in execute_plan
    result = await method(*step.args)
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/juju/model.py", line 1927, in deploy
    storage=storage,
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/juju/model.py", line 1299, in _deploy
    result = await app_facade.Deploy([app])
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/juju/client/facade.py", line 412, in wrapper
    reply = await f(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/juju/client/_client5.py", line 503, in Deploy
    reply = await self.rpc(msg)
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/juju/client/facade.py", line 537, in rpc
    result = await self.connection.rpc(msg, encoder=TypeEncoder)
  File "/snap/conjure-up/987/lib/python3.6/site-packages/juju/client/connection.py", line 314, in rpc
    raise errors.JujuAPIError(result)
juju.errors.JujuAPIError: json: cannot unmarshal string into Go struct field Value.tags of type []string
```

This is due to the constraints parsing [only creating a list if there is a comma](https://github.com/juju/python-libjuju/blob/master/juju/constraints.py#L75) whereas it needs to happen always for `tags` and `spaces`.

issue_title: Can't delete bundles in worker_offline state
PayIn.get() doesn't delegate to subclass
issue_body: In v2 `payIns.Get(id)` returned an object containing all the data provided by the API, albeit in an annoying hierarchy. In v3 `PayIn.get()` doesn't delegate to its subclasses (`DirectPayIn`, `BankWirePayIn`, etc), so only attributes common to all types of payins are accessible, and the rest of the data provided by the API is discarded. This means that getting a proper object requires using the correct subclass directly, which isn't possible when handling a webhook notification because the querystring doesn't contain the payin type.

issue_title: Ability to terminate a SAS session from within an ipynb?
issue_body:
Hi.  I love the saspy package.  It is a great addition to the SAS System.  I have searched the docs and can't find a way to terminate a SAS session from within an ipynb.  I found the article about disconnecting from a network and then reconnecting to a running SAS session.  That is very useful.   But, it would also be useful to be able to TERMINATE a connection to a remote SAS server from within an ipynb.  I am asking since we have seen instances where stopping the Python kernel, and closing the ipynb does NOT terminate the running remote SAS session on our Linux SAS GRID.

Are there any plans to add a new command to the saspy package that would allow us to terminate a remote SAS session?  Or, perhaps this already exists and I've just missed it?  ;-)

issue_title: Validate web3 signed message
issue_body:
Is it possible to use `eth-keys` to validate web3 (Metamask backend) signed messages?

I've been trying to piece it together from documentation, but no luck so far.
https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/wiki/Management-APIs#personal_sign
https://github.com/ethereum/wiki/wiki/JavaScript-API#web3ethsign

Code (only guessing so far):
```
def recover_address(data: str, signature: str) -> str:
    signature = binascii.unhexlify(remove_0x_prefix(signature))
    data = keccak(text=data)
    data = f"\\x19Ethereum Signed Message:\n{len(data)}{data}"
    data = bytes(data, 'utf-8')

    # web3js outputs in rsv order
    vrs = (
        ord(signature[64:65]) - 27,
        big_endian_to_int(signature[0:32]),
        big_endian_to_int(signature[32:64]),
    )
    sig = KeyAPI.Signature(vrs=vrs)
    return sig.recover_public_key_from_msg(data).to_address()
```

Web3js sign example:
```
let address = ''0xDD61fA8CEd6F49e70c3b9fD45F2Ed6017128a6Fd';
web3.eth.sign(address, web3.sha3('foo'), (e, r) => console.log(r))
```

Expectation:
```
recover_address('foo', r)
# should output 0xDD61fA8CEd6F49e70c3b9fD45F2Ed6017128a6Fd
```

issue_title: Add ability to process itsdb profiles
issue_body:
The ACE and web clients allow a user to process a single item or an iterable of items. I would like to also process a profile. This could be done in at least two ways, of which one should be chosen:

* add a parse_profile() function (and generate_profile(), etc.) to the ace module.
  ```python
  ace.parse_profile(grm, prof)
  ```
* add a process() method on the ItsdbProfile class.
  ```python
  profile.process(aceparser)
  ```

The benefit to the former is that it follows the current pattern. The benefit to the latter is that it opens the door to other processors (e.g. httpparser, agreeparser, etc.).

These functions need to know what table and field to process (whether it's item:i-input for parsing, result:mrs for transfer and generation, or something else for non-standard schemata). It should also be possible to specify a source profile separate from the profile to be written (particularly for transfer and generation for the standard schema). Maybe it could be possible to specify a source corpus and to receive the results as from the standard ace.parse() functions. And maybe I could rework those ParseResult and ParseResponse classes into the in-memory profiles (see #58).

pytest                    3.0.5                    py27_0    mirror
